metadata.title: Overview of comments received on 'Guideline on the evaluation of medicinal products for the treatment of chronic constipation (including opioid induced constipation) and for bowel cleansing' (EMA/CHMP/336243/2013)
metadata.notes: Rules extracted exclusively from CHMP Outcome column positions. Stakeholder comments are not treated as rules. Many rows have purely procedural outcomes (Agreed/minor wording change) without substantive regulatory position — only outcomes stating a CHMP regulatory stance are captured.

rules.rule_text: For an OIC indication, efficacy in cancer pain patients may be justified by documentation of efficacy in a sufficiently large population of non-cancer pain patients treated with high doses of opioids. A separate documentation of safety pre-approval in cancer pain patients is still required. Separate confirmatory efficacy trials in the cancer-pain population are only necessary where a sufficiently large number of patients with high opioid doses in the non-cancer pain population has not been documented, or where a company has decided to investigate cancer pain patients in the first place.
rules.verbatim_quote: Partly agreed. The notion that efficacy in cancer pain patients can be justified by documentation of efficacy in a sufficiently large population of non-cancer pain patients treated with high doses of opioids is now included in the GL. The conduct of a documentation of safety pre-approval is, however, still included. The following recommendations regarding the conduct of efficacy trials in the cancer-pain population is therefore only for the cases where as sufficiently large number of patient with high doses in the non-cancer pain population has not been documented, or in case a company has decided to investigate cancer pain patients in the first place.
exceptions: If a company elects to investigate cancer pain patients directly, full confirmatory efficacy trials in that population are required regardless of non-cancer pain data.

rules.rule_text: When describing the relationship between OIC response rates and opioid dose, the guideline language must reflect scientific uncertainty; the characterisation that higher opioid doses lead to lower response rates 'could be suspected' but is not definitive.
rules.source_section: Section 2 — Specific comments on text, Lines 321-323
rules.verbatim_quote: Partly agreed. Wording has been changed to "could be suspected".

rules.rule_text: For OIC studies aiming at a first-line indication, both subgroups of new previously untreated patients and those with a recorded inadequate response to standard laxatives must be sufficiently powered to allow for the assessment of efficacy in those groups. The advice to adequately power these subgroups applies generally, not only to OIC.
rules.source_section: Section 2 — Specific comments on text, Lines 486-487
rules.verbatim_quote: Agreed. However, the advice to adequately power the studies has been inserted earlier in the text (after previous line 188) where the problem of "previous failed therapy" is dealt with in a more general way, because this might not only be applicable to OIC.

rules.rule_text: Broadening the OIC indication from chronic idiopathic constipation (CIC) to chronic constipation in general is considered possible. An OIC indication must not be restricted to µ-opioid antagonists specifically; if relevant effects are shown for such treatments, the indication should also be granted for compounds with a different mechanism of action. Claims for secondary constipation alone (or parts thereof) are undesirable.
rules.source_section: Section 2 — Specific comments on text (Stakeholder 3, general comment on OIC scope)
rules.verbatim_quote: Partially agreed only. It is agreed that an indication of secondary constipation alone (or even parts of secondary constipation) are undesirable. However, the broadening of the indication from CIC to chronic constipation in general is considered to be possible. Respective changes have been included. Also, a restriction of the OIC-indication to specific treatments (the µ-opioid antagonists) is not considered adequate. If relevant effects are shown for such treatments, the indication should also be granted for compounds with a different MoA.

rules.rule_text: Inclusion of European patients in OIC clinical trials is considered necessary for safety documentation, based on different prescribing patterns in Europe compared with the US, and the potentially 'milder' diseased population of non-malignant pain patients in the US.
rules.source_section: Section 2 — Specific comments on text (Stakeholder 3, European data requirement)
rules.verbatim_quote: This is not agreed with. For the sake of safety documentation, the inclusion of European patients is considered necessary based on the different prescribing patterns, and a potentially "milder" diseased population of non-malignant pain patients in the US.

rules.rule_text: The conditions under which a separate documentation of efficacy in cancer pain patients is required for an OIC indication have been specified. Refer to Chapter 5.1.2 of the guideline for those conditions.
rules.source_section: Section 2 — Specific comments on text (Stakeholder 3, separate confirmatory trials in cancer pain)
rules.verbatim_quote: Partially agreed. The conditions under which a separate documentation of efficacy would be necessary for cancer pain patients have been specified (See Chapter 5.1.2.)

rules.rule_text: A specific claim for 'secondary constipation' as a standalone indication is no longer included in the guideline and is declared unwanted. The recommendation to conduct separate studies for secondary constipation has been changed from 'in all circumstances' to 'usually'. Including the secondary constipation population as subgroups within broader studies is now an accepted option.
rules.source_section: Section 2 — Specific comments on text, Lines 145-149 (Stakeholder 3)
rules.verbatim_quote: Partially agreed. A potential claim for "secondary constipation" is now no longer included (and declared unwanted), and the recommendation to conduct separate studies is changed from "in all circumstances" to "usually". A possibility to include the secondary population as subgroups has also been added.
exceptions: Separate studies for secondary constipation may still be required in specific circumstances at CHMP discretion.

rules.rule_text: Digital rectal examination to detect increased sphincter tone (for exclusion of dyssynergia) is retained as a patient selection criterion for Phase II/III trials in chronic constipation. This method is considered to have high diagnostic accuracy and is the most suitable for use in those trial phases.
rules.source_section: Section 2 — Specific comments on text, Line 270 (Stakeholder 3)
rules.verbatim_quote: Not agreed. This simple method has shown a high diagnostic accuracy in studies and is so easy to perform that it is considered most suitable for used in Phase II/III

rules.rule_text: A compulsory 6-month treatment duration in the controlled phase of chronic constipation trials is hard to justify as an absolute requirement. Applicants must document the need for re-treatment where applicable.
rules.source_section: Section 2 — Specific comments on text, Line 452 (Stakeholder 3)
rules.verbatim_quote: Partially agreed. However, a compulsory 6-month treatment requirement is hard to justify. The need to document re-treatment has also been added.

rules.rule_text: If an active comparator is included in a chronic constipation or OIC trial, either superiority against the active comparator must be shown, or a placebo arm must be included to guarantee assay sensitivity. A three-arm trial (investigational compound, active comparator, placebo) is recommended where non-inferiority is the goal. Simple documentation of superiority to placebo using an active comparator solely for 'assay sensitivity' is not recommended.
rules.source_section: Section 2 — Specific comments on text (Stakeholder 3, assay sensitivity / comparator design)
rules.verbatim_quote: Not agreed. The intention was to recommend against a mere documentation of "assay sensitivity", but to request for non-inferiority also, or for the documentation of other advantages if a 3-arm trial is performed. No changes implemented.
exceptions: If both active controls clearly separate from placebo in a three-arm trial, the constraints for formally showing non-inferiority are less stringent.

rules.rule_text: A separate standalone guideline for bowel cleansing agents was not considered appropriate at the time of guideline finalisation. Bowel cleansing is addressed within the chronic constipation guideline due to similarities in compounds used for CIC and bowel cleansing indications.
rules.source_section: Section 2 — Specific comments on text (Stakeholder 4, request for separate bowel cleansing guideline)
rules.verbatim_quote: Not agreed. It is acknowledged that bowel cleansing is a topic relevantly different from CIC and OIC. However, the similarities in compounds used, especially for the CIC indication gave the chance to generate the guidance at this point of time. The need for some guidance in the field also appears to be obvious. However, at the current point of time, a separate guidance was not felt appropriate.

rules.rule_text: The bowel cleansing guideline covers agents with potential for application beyond colonoscopy preparation (e.g. other diagnostic or surgical procedures), but a specific need to enumerate additional indications beyond colonoscopy has not been identified. Other indications may be considered if adequate justification is presented.
rules.source_section: Section 2 — Specific comments on text, Lines 197-200 (Stakeholder 4)
rules.verbatim_quote: Not agreed. The current proposals include the possibility of other indications, if an adequate justification is presented. A need for mentioning these other indications is not identified.

rules.rule_text: Extrapolation of pharmacokinetic data from adults to the paediatric population through modelling is not accepted for inclusion in the guideline. No issues have been identified with the general rules for PK documentation in the paediatric population that would require statements on PK extrapolation.
rules.source_section: Section 2 — Specific comments on text, Line 667 (Stakeholder 4)
rules.verbatim_quote: Not agreed. Currently there is no reflection of PK documentation included. No issues have been identified that go beyond the "general" rules for PK documentation in the paediatric population. Therefore, the need to include statements on extrapolation is not accepted.

rules.rule_text: For OIC trials in the non-malignant pain population, efficacy must be demonstrated in a controlled manner for at least 3 months. This minimum duration is required because treatment in this population is theoretically unrestricted in duration.
rules.source_section: Section 2 — Specific comments on text, Line 506 (Stakeholder 5)
rules.verbatim_quote: Not agreed. The studies with Naloxegol have shown to have 3 months durations with acceptable drop-out rates (16-19%)... In the non-malignant pain population, there exists a clear need to show efficacy in controlled manner for at least 3 months due to the (theoretically) unrestricted treatment duration in this patient population.
exceptions: Shorter placebo-controlled duration may be acceptable for drugs with targeted mechanisms where drop-out rates and blinding integrity can be maintained.

rules.rule_text: Primary endpoints for OIC trials must reflect patient well-being and the relevant features of the underlying disease, not merely pharmacodynamic activity of the compound. Endpoints that capture only the timing of laxation after dosing or influence on associated symptoms alone are not accepted as primary endpoints for OIC studies.
rules.source_section: Section 2 — Specific comments on text, Lines 544-548 (Stakeholder 5)
rules.verbatim_quote: Not agreed. The proposed endpoints are regarded to correlate to the pharmacodynamic activity of a compound only, but not to the well-being of the patient, or the relevant features of the underlying disease. It is considered that for a patient it does not matter whether laxation takes place immediately after drug administration or in a delayed manner only. Also, an influence on the associated symptoms would also not be covered by the proposal.

rules.rule_text: The Bowel Function Index (BFI) is not accepted as a primary endpoint for OIC trials. BFI is accepted as a secondary efficacy variable. The BFI has two unresolved problems as primary endpoint: (1) high risk of recall bias because symptoms are recorded weekly only; and (2) it is physician-administered rather than directly patient-centred. There is currently no fully validated outcome measure for OIC; other primary endpoints with adequate justification and available validation data may be considered.
rules.source_section: Section 2 — Specific comments on text, Lines 524ff (Stakeholder 6)
rules.verbatim_quote: Not agreed. The use of the BFI as primary endpoint, and an evaluation of the publicly available validation data had been done for the draft guideline already... However, there are two unresolved problems with the BFI that stood against the choice/acceptance as primary endpoint... There appears to be a high risk of "recall bias" in as the symptoms are recorded once weekly only... Moreover, the questionnaire is not directly patient centered but filled in by the physician... However, the BFI is considered to be a valuable tool in the assessment of treatment effects in the disease and therefore explicitly mentioned as secondary efficacy variable. It must be acknowledged (and is included in the guideline) that there is currently no fully validated outcome measure. Hence a paragraph that does in principle allow the use of other primary endpoints with adequate justification and presentation of available validation data is additionally included.
exceptions: Other primary endpoints with adequate justification and presentation of available validation data may be used in place of the BFI.
